statement: Next.js is built around a hybrid rendering philosophy: the same codebase can serve pages via Static Site Generation (SSG, built at compile time), Server-Side Rendering (SSR, rendered per request), Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR, statically served and revalidated in the background after a configurable interval), and Streaming (App Router progressive HTML flushing via React Suspense). The correct choice is determined per route by data freshness requirements and personalization needs, not by a global setting. SSG/ISR is preferred for performance and cost; SSR is reserved for pages that require request-time data (cookies, auth headers); Streaming reduces TTFB for slow data.

The App Router uses a file-system convention where the directory structure under app/ defines routes. Special filenames have fixed semantics: page.tsx renders the route UI, layout.tsx wraps a route segment and its children persistently, loading.tsx is an automatic Suspense boundary shown during navigation, error.tsx is an Error Boundary for that segment, not-found.tsx handles notFound() calls, and route.ts defines HTTP API handlers. Colocation of components, tests, and styles is allowed inside route segments because only files named with these reserved names are exposed as routes. This replaces the pages/ directory convention from the Pages Router.

Adding 'use client' to a component marks it and all its imported subtree as Client Components, opting the entire branch out of server rendering and React Server Components benefits (zero bundle size, direct data access, no hydration cost). The common antipattern is placing 'use client' at a high level in the tree (e.g., a layout or large feature component) because one child needs onClick or useState, when the correct fix is to extract only the interactive leaf into its own Client Component. Overuse of 'use client' defeats the App Router's performance model, bloats the JS bundle sent to the browser, and prevents async Server Components from fetching data at the point of use.

Next.js App Router caches aggressively by default at four layers: the Request Memoization cache (per-render deduplication), the Data Cache (persistent across requests, opt-out with cache:'no-store' or revalidate:0), the Full Route Cache (static HTML stored on disk/CDN), and the Router Cache (client-side segment cache). The failure mode is deploying dynamic data fetches without explicit cache opt-out and seeing stale data served indefinitely. Common symptoms: API responses not updating after writes, POST Server Actions not invalidating GET data, new deployments still serving old content. Fix by calling revalidatePath() / revalidateTag() in Server Actions after mutations, or setting fetch({ next: { revalidate: N } }) appropriately.

Server Actions are POST endpoints automatically created by Next.js for functions marked with 'use server'. Security implications: (1) they are publicly reachable HTTP endpoints — any user can call them directly with a crafted request, so authorization checks (session validation, RBAC) must be inside the action body, not only in the UI that invokes them; (2) Server Action IDs are exposed in the client bundle and can be enumerated; (3) input must be validated server-side (use zod or similar) because Client Components pass arguments over the network; (4) CSRF protection is built in via the Origin header check in Next.js ≥ 14.1, but only when invoked via form — direct fetch calls bypass this.

Next.js provides three request-handling primitives with distinct execution contexts and capabilities. Middleware (middleware.ts at project root) runs on the Edge Runtime before routing on every matched request — it can read/modify request headers and cookies and redirect/rewrite, but cannot access Node.js APIs, databases, or large npm packages. Route Handlers (app/*/route.ts) run in the Node.js runtime (or Edge if opted in) and are full HTTP handlers supporting all methods; they replace API Routes from the Pages Router. API Routes (pages/api/*.ts) are the Pages Router equivalent. Use middleware for auth redirects and A/B testing header injection; use Route Handlers for REST endpoints and webhook receivers; never put heavy computation in middleware.

A Next.js App Router route segment is statically rendered (at build time) unless it opts into dynamic rendering. Dynamic rendering is triggered automatically when the segment uses: cookies(), headers(), or searchParams (dynamic APIs that are request-specific), or a fetch() with cache:'no-store' or revalidate:0. If any component in the segment tree triggers dynamic rendering, the entire segment becomes dynamic. The decision tree: prefer static + ISR (revalidate interval) by default; use Partial Prerendering (PPR, experimental) to statically shell with streaming dynamic islands; fall back to full dynamic only when personalization or real-time data is mandatory.

next/image automatically handles responsive sizing (srcset generation), lazy loading, format conversion to WebP/AVIF, and prevents layout shift by requiring width/height or fill props. The critical configuration points: the 'domains' or 'remotePatterns' allowlist in next.config.js must explicitly permit external image hostnames, and omitting 'priority' on above-the-fold images will defer loading and hurt LCP. next/font (Google Fonts and local fonts) eliminates the external network request at runtime by embedding font CSS at build time and uses font-display:swap by default, preventing render-blocking font loads. Both optimizations require no CDN setup and work in self-hosted deployments.

Parallel Routes (@folder convention) allow rendering multiple independent route segments in the same layout slot simultaneously, each with its own loading and error states. They are used for dashboards with independently loading panels, or conditional UI (e.g., show modal OR full page based on navigation context). Intercepting Routes ((.) and (..) conventions) allow a route to render inside the current layout when navigated to via client-side navigation, but as a standalone page when accessed directly or on refresh — the primary use case is photo gallery modals that show as overlays during browsing but as full pages when shared via URL. These two patterns are often composed together (modal routes).

Deploying Next.js on Vercel vs. self-hosted has significant capability and operational differences. Vercel provides native support for all Next.js features (ISR, Edge Middleware, Image Optimization, Server Actions, Streaming) with zero configuration, automatic CDN revalidation via revalidateTag(), and preview deployments per branch. Self-hosted deployments (Docker with 'output:standalone', or node server.js) support all Node.js runtime features but require manual CDN setup for static asset caching, a separate image optimization service or relying on built-in server-side optimization without a CDN, and manual cache revalidation plumbing. Edge Middleware in self-hosted mode requires an edge-compatible reverse proxy (e.g., Cloudflare Workers). The standalone output mode bundles only the required node_modules for minimal Docker image size.

Next.js App Router Server Components cannot use React hooks (useState, useEffect, useContext) or browser-only APIs (window, document, localStorage) because they execute exclusively on the server. The subtle failure mode is that adding a hook to a server component produces a build-time error, but importing a third-party library that internally uses hooks (e.g., a UI component library) inside a Server Component causes the same error in a hard-to-trace way. The diagnostic is: find the chain of imports from the Server Component to the code calling the hook. Fix either by wrapping the third-party component in a 'use client' leaf wrapper, or by lazy-loading the component with next/dynamic and ssr:false if it is purely decorative and not needed for initial render.

Next.js Partial Prerendering (PPR, available as experimental since Next 14) splits a route into a static shell rendered at build time and dynamic holes that stream in per-request via Suspense boundaries. The mental model: wrap slow-loading personalized content in <Suspense fallback={...}> and the framework serves the outer shell instantly from CDN while the dynamic parts stream. The constraint is that PPR requires all dynamic APIs (cookies(), headers()) to be called inside Suspense boundaries — using them outside causes the entire route to fall back to full dynamic rendering, silently negating the optimization. Enable with `experimental: { ppr: true }` and audit with `next build --debug` output showing route rendering modes.

Next.js middleware running on the Edge Runtime has a 1MB code size limit (compressed) and cannot import Node.js native modules (fs, crypto, net, etc.), use npm packages that depend on Node built-ins, or execute long-running synchronous work. A common production failure is importing a JWT validation library that transitively depends on the Node.js crypto module, causing middleware to fail silently or throw at the edge. The fix is to use the Web Crypto API (available in Edge Runtime via globalThis.crypto) for cryptographic operations, or use jose for JWT handling as it is Edge-compatible. Always test middleware in edge preview mode locally before deploying.

Next.js App Router provides the generateStaticParams function for pre-rendering dynamic routes at build time. The key behavior: for a route like app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx, returning an array of { slug } objects from generateStaticParams() causes Next.js to statically generate one HTML page per combination. Combined with revalidate, this enables ISR for dynamic segments — the page is statically served and regenerated in the background. The performance implication: for sites with thousands of pages, generating all params at build time is slow; set dynamicParams = true (default) and only generate the most critical pages in generateStaticParams, allowing less-visited pages to be rendered on first request and then cached.

Next.js instrumentation.ts (stable since Next 15) allows running code once when the server starts, before any requests are served. This is the correct place for: (1) OpenTelemetry SDK initialization; (2) registering global error trackers (Sentry.init); (3) warming caches or connections at startup. The file must export a register() function at the root of the project (not inside app/ or pages/). Before instrumentation.ts was available, teams used _app.tsx or middleware for this, causing either double-initialization on hot reloads or edge-runtime incompatibility. The register() function runs in both Node.js and Edge runtimes, so guard Node-specific initialization with process.env.NEXT_RUNTIME === 'nodejs'.

Next.js generateMetadata function (App Router) enables dynamic SEO metadata including Open Graph, Twitter cards, and canonical URLs. The critical pattern is that generateMetadata is a Server Component async function that runs on the server per-request for dynamic routes, so it can fetch from databases or external APIs without client-side rendering. For static routes, return a plain metadata object instead. A common mistake is duplicating metadata in both the layout and page — layout metadata is merged with page metadata, with page taking precedence on conflict. For i18n sites, use the alternates.languages map to emit hreflang tags, and set metadataBase in the root layout to resolve relative image URLs correctly for social sharing previews.

Calling cookies() or headers() inside a Server Component that is exported with revalidate > 0 or from a generateStaticParams route causes a Next.js build error because static generation cannot depend on request-time values. The failure is confusing because the error message points to the dynamic API call, not the revalidate configuration. The root design issue: you must choose between static (deterministic at build time) and dynamic (per-request) rendering — you cannot have both. The fix is either remove revalidate to make the route fully dynamic, or restructure to move the cookies()/headers() call into a separate Client Component or a different Server Component that is explicitly opt-in to dynamic rendering via `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'`.

Testing Next.js App Router applications requires strategy adaptations compared to Pages Router. Unit testing Server Components directly is not yet officially supported by React Testing Library (as of early 2025) because RSC cannot run in jsdom. The recommended strategy is: (1) test Server Component data-fetching logic in isolation by extracting it to pure async functions testable without the component; (2) use Playwright or Cypress for integration tests that exercise actual rendering; (3) use react-testing-library + jest/vitest for Client Components only; (4) for API routes, test with supertest or fetch against a running Next.js server. The msw (Mock Service Worker) library works in both browser and Node.js contexts for mocking fetch calls in tests.

Next.js bundle analysis via @next/bundle-analyzer is the primary tool for diagnosing client-side JavaScript weight. Run ANALYZE=true next build to generate an interactive treemap showing what is in each JS chunk. Common findings that warrant action: (1) date-fns or lodash imported without tree-shaking (use date-fns/format instead of date-fns); (2) large third-party UI libraries imported wholesale; (3) code that should be server-only accidentally included in client bundles. The server-only package (import 'server-only') throws a build-time error if a file is ever imported in a Client Component, making it the correct guard for server-side secrets and large server dependencies that must not ship to browsers.

Next.js environment variable handling has three tiers with strict client exposure rules. Variables without a prefix are only available server-side (process.env.DB_URL — never sent to browser). Variables prefixed NEXT_PUBLIC_ are inlined at build time into the client bundle — they are baked into the JavaScript and cannot change without a rebuild, and they are fully visible to anyone who inspects the page source. For runtime server configuration that changes per deployment without a rebuild, use runtime env vars accessed only in server code or via a /api route. The common security failure is putting secrets in NEXT_PUBLIC_ because they happened to also be needed client-side — the correct fix is to expose only a derived non-secret value via a server endpoint.

Next.js App Router: importing a Client Component from a Server Component must be done carefully. If the Client Component uses client-only features (hooks, event handlers) and is imported without 'use client', Next.js may fail to render correctly or produce hydration errors. The 'use client' directive marks the module boundary — everything imported by a 'use client' file also becomes client code. UNSAID: the propagation direction matters. 'use client' propagates DOWN (all imports of a client component become client code), NOT UP. A server component CAN import a client component but cannot pass server-only data (database results directly) as props — serialize to JSON first. Server components cannot be async and return client component instances that themselves are async.

Next.js App Router data fetching in Server Components: `async function Page({ params }) { const data = await db.query(params.id); return <Component data={data} /> }`. No useEffect, no loading state, no API route needed. The component is an async function that awaits data directly. UNSAID: this is the paradigm shift — Server Components enable the query-to-HTML pattern that PHP/Rails always had. The fetch() function in Next.js Server Components is extended with automatic deduplication (same URL fetched in parallel by multiple components sends one request) and caching options. `fetch(url, { cache: 'force-cache' })` for static (CDN-cached), `{ next: { revalidate: 3600 } }` for ISR, `{ cache: 'no-store' }` for SSR (always fresh).

Next.js middleware runs on the Edge runtime — it has NO access to Node.js APIs (fs, path, crypto.createHash). Middleware must use Web APIs only (fetch, Response, Request, Headers, cookies, URL). Attempting to import Node.js-only modules in middleware causes build errors. UNSAID: this is the most common middleware error for developers moving from Pages Router (where middleware had full Node.js access). Edge runtime middleware runs globally distributed in Cloudflare/Vercel's edge network — that's why it must use Web APIs. For operations requiring Node.js (database queries, bcrypt), use API routes with the Node.js runtime, not middleware. Middleware should only: redirect, rewrite, add headers, and check cookies/JWT signatures.

Next.js App Router layout.tsx: layouts wrap page content and persist across navigations in the same route segment. The root layout (app/layout.tsx) must contain html and body tags. Nested layouts compose — (dashboard)/layout.tsx renders inside the root layout's children slot. UNSAID: layouts maintain their state across navigations within the same segment. If the dashboard layout fetches user data in onMounted/useEffect, it won't refetch on navigation between dashboard pages — it's the same component instance. Server layouts can fetch data: `async function DashboardLayout({ children }) { const user = await getUser(); return <><Sidebar user={user}/>{children}</> }`. This is superior to fetching user data in every page.

Next.js Image component: missing the `alt` prop fails accessibility requirements. Missing `priority` on above-the-fold images causes poor Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) scores. Using `fill` without a positioned parent (position: relative or absolute) causes the image to render at 0x0. UNSAID: the `sizes` prop is critical for responsive performance — without it, Next.js generates one image size and uses it for all viewport widths. With `sizes='(max-width: 768px) 100vw, 50vw'`, Next.js generates optimized images for each breakpoint and uses srcset. Missing `sizes` means mobile devices download desktop-sized images. The `quality` prop defaults to 75 — appropriate for most cases but reduce to 60 for background images.

Next.js App Router parallel routes: `@folder` syntax creates parallel route slots rendered simultaneously. Use case: dashboard with main content and side panel both independently fetchable. `app/@modal/(.)photo/[id]/page.tsx` creates an intercepting route that shows as a modal overlay. UNSAID: parallel routes and intercepting routes enable the 'Modal on the same URL' pattern — clicking a gallery image shows it in a modal (with URL change for sharing), but navigating directly to the URL shows the full image page. This is the pattern used by Pinterest, Twitter, and Instagram for photo modals. The `default.tsx` file provides the fallback when a slot has no active match — prevents undefined slot errors on hard navigation.

Next.js Server Actions with form validation: define server action with 'use server', validate with Zod, return structured error response. `useFormState` hook on client manages the form state including server-side validation errors. Pattern: client submits form → server action validates → returns { errors: ZodError | null, data: T | null } → useFormState updates UI. UNSAID: server actions are the Next.js 14+ replacement for API routes for mutations. They provide: automatic CSRF protection (SameSite cookie + Origin header check), progressive enhancement (works without JavaScript), and TypeScript types without manual API contract. The security consideration: server actions are HTTP POST endpoints — validate ALL inputs server-side regardless of client-side validation.

Next.js revalidatePath and revalidateTag: after a server action mutation, call `revalidatePath('/dashboard')` to purge the cached page and force re-render on next request. `revalidateTag('users')` purges all fetches tagged with `{ next: { tags: ['users'] } }`. UNSAID: forgetting to call revalidatePath/revalidateTag after mutations causes stale data — the page cache still shows the old data. This is the most common App Router mutation bug. The common pattern: create user → revalidatePath('/users') → redirect('/users'). The redirect() from next/navigation throws a special NEXT_REDIRECT error — it must NOT be called inside try/catch (the catch block swallows the redirect). Call redirect() outside of try/catch, in the success path only.

Next.js App Router dynamic routing: `[id]` folder creates a dynamic segment, `[...slug]` is a catch-all (required slug), `[[...slug]]` is an optional catch-all. Route groups `(folder)` organize routes without affecting the URL. Private folders `_folder` are excluded from routing. UNSAID: route groups enable multiple root layouts within the app — `(marketing)/layout.tsx` and `(dashboard)/layout.tsx` can have completely different layouts at the same URL depth. This is essential for apps with different authenticated vs. public experiences. The `(folder)` name never appears in the URL — it's purely organizational. Dynamic segments are available as `params` prop in page components: `page.tsx` receives `{ params: { id: string } }`.

Next.js route handlers (route.ts): API routes in App Router. HTTP method handlers: `export async function GET(request, { params }) {}`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, etc. Return `Response` or `NextResponse`. UNSAID: route handlers in App Router replace pages/api/ routes from Pages Router. The key difference: App Router route handlers are web-standard — they use the Web Fetch API (Request, Response) instead of Node.js req/res. `NextResponse` adds Next.js-specific features (cookies, redirects with full Next.js context). For reading request body: `await request.json()`. For streaming responses: `return new Response(readableStream)`. Route handlers are also affected by the Partial Prerendering static/dynamic boundary.

Next.js cookies() and headers() are async in Next.js 15: `const cookieStore = await cookies()`, `const headersList = await headers()`. In Next.js 14 and earlier, these were synchronous. Upgrading to Next.js 15 requires adding await to all cookies/headers calls — missing await causes TypeScript errors and runtime bugs. UNSAID: this breaking change in Next.js 15 was controversial — adding await everywhere is tedious. Next.js 15 provides a codemod: `npx @next/codemod@latest next-async-request-api` to automatically add awaits. The reason for the change: Dynamic APIs (cookies, headers, searchParams) must be async to enable Partial Prerendering — static and dynamic parts of a page are rendered separately, requiring async boundaries.

Next.js Metadata API: export `metadata` object or `generateMetadata` async function from page.tsx/layout.tsx. `export const metadata: Metadata = { title: 'Home', description: '...' }`. For dynamic metadata: `export async function generateMetadata({ params }) { const product = await getProduct(params.id); return { title: product.name } }`. UNSAID: generateMetadata runs on the server — it can fetch data from databases or APIs. Next.js deduplicates requests between generateMetadata and page components using React's cache(). Open Graph images: `export const metadata = { openGraph: { images: ['/og-image.png'] } }`. Dynamic OG images via `app/opengraph-image.tsx` using @vercel/og (Satori) generate PNG from React components server-side.

Next.js ISR revalidation: `revalidate: 60` means pages are served from cache and revalidated in the background every 60 seconds. NOT: revalidated every 60 seconds. The actual behavior: first request after 60 seconds triggers background revalidation — the current request still gets the old cache. New content appears on the NEXT request after revalidation completes. UNSAID: this stale-while-revalidate behavior is intentional — it keeps response times fast (always serve from cache) while keeping content fresh. For truly fresh content, use `cache: 'no-store'` (SSR). ISR is not appropriate for: checkout pages (need accurate pricing), user-specific pages (serve stale data to wrong user), or security-sensitive pages. ISR is ideal for: blog posts, product listings, marketing pages.

Next.js App Router with Prisma: database queries in Server Components directly. `const users = await prisma.user.findMany()` inside an async Server Component. No API route needed for read operations. For mutations: Server Actions call Prisma, then revalidatePath/revalidateTag, then redirect. UNSAID: Prisma Client is a singleton in Next.js — instantiating per request causes connection pool exhaustion. The standard pattern: `const prisma = global.prisma ?? new PrismaClient(); if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') global.prisma = prisma`. This reuses the same instance across hot-reloads in development. In production, Prisma Client auto-manages the connection pool. Prisma Accelerate (connection pooling for serverless) is required for Vercel/Edge deployments where database connections can't be persistent.

Next.js authentication with NextAuth.js (Auth.js v5): configure providers (Google, GitHub, credentials) in auth.ts. Export auth, signIn, signOut, handlers. Route handler in app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts. Middleware protects routes. `const session = await auth()` in Server Components gets the session. UNSAID: Auth.js v5 (NextAuth v5) is the rewrite for App Router. Auth.js v4 (NextAuth v4) was Pages Router. The migration from v4 to v5 is significant — different import paths, different session shape, different configuration. v5 introduces the Auth middleware pattern that protects entire route segments with a single middleware.ts file. Credentials provider (username/password): avoid if possible — it requires implementing password hashing, session management, and security controls that OAuth providers handle automatically.

Next.js Partial Prerendering (PPR, Next.js 14+ experimental): static and dynamic parts of a page are rendered separately. The outer shell is statically generated; Suspense boundaries wrap dynamic content that renders per-request. Enabling PPR changes caching behavior — misplaced Suspense boundaries cause unexpected static content. UNSAID: PPR is the future of Next.js rendering — replacing the binary SSG vs SSR choice with a hybrid at the component level. The mental model: everything outside Suspense boundaries is static (cached at build time), everything inside Suspense is dynamic (rendered per request). This is opt-in per route with `export const experimental_ppr = true`. The implication for architecture: structure components so expensive dynamic sections are inside Suspense, enabling the static shell to be CDN-cached globally.

Next.js font optimization: `next/font` eliminates font-related layout shift (CLS) and avoids external network requests for fonts. `import { Inter } from 'next/font/google'; const inter = Inter({ subsets: ['latin'] })`. Apply to body: `<body className={inter.className}>`. UNSAID: next/font downloads Google Fonts at build time and self-hosts them — no Google font requests from the browser (privacy benefit + performance benefit). Variable fonts reduce the number of font files. The `display: 'swap'` behavior is the default — text renders in system font until the custom font loads, then swaps. For font subsets: specify only the subsets your content uses (e.g., 'latin' not 'latin-ext') to minimize download size. Local fonts: `next/font/local` supports `woff2` files — optimal format for modern browsers.

Next.js environment variables: `NEXT_PUBLIC_` prefix exposes variables to the browser. Non-prefixed variables are server-only. Accessing a non-prefixed variable in a Client Component returns undefined — no error thrown. UNSAID: this silent undefined is the most insidious Next.js environment variable bug. The fix: rename to `NEXT_PUBLIC_VAR` for client-accessible variables. Security consideration: `NEXT_PUBLIC_` variables are baked into the JavaScript bundle — they're visible in browser devtools and in the source map. Never put secrets (API keys, tokens) in `NEXT_PUBLIC_` variables. For client-side API calls that need secrets, proxy through a Next.js API route that has the secret in a non-public env var.

Next.js static generation with generateStaticParams: pre-generates dynamic route pages at build time. `export async function generateStaticParams() { const posts = await getPosts(); return posts.map(p => ({ slug: p.slug })) }`. Combined with `fetch({ cache: 'force-cache' })`, builds a fully static site with dynamic URLs. UNSAID: generateStaticParams is the App Router equivalent of getStaticPaths in Pages Router. The fallback behavior: pages not in generateStaticParams are rendered on-demand on first request (equivalent to `fallback: 'blocking'`). Setting `export const dynamicParams = false` makes Next.js return 404 for paths not generated at build time — useful for content where all valid paths are known upfront (finite blog posts, product catalog).

Next.js App Router and Pages Router coexistence during migration: both app/ and pages/ can coexist in one project. app/ takes precedence for matching routes — if both app/about/page.tsx and pages/about.tsx exist, app/ wins. Middleware applies to both. UNSAID: the migration strategy is incremental — migrate routes one by one from pages/ to app/ while both coexist. Shared components don't need to change immediately. The risk during coexistence: data fetching patterns are completely different (getServerSideProps in Pages Router vs async Server Components in App Router) — don't mix them in the same component. API routes in pages/api/ continue working alongside app/ route handlers. The migration is complete when the pages/ directory is empty.

Next.js with TanStack Query and Server Components: prefetch query on server, dehydrate state, serialize with Hydrate on client. Pattern: Server Component fetches and prefetches using queryClient.prefetchQuery, dehydrateState passed as prop to Client Component, HydrationBoundary wraps the Client Component. UNSAID: this pattern eliminates the loading flash on initial page load — data is prefetched on the server and available immediately on client. The client-side TanStack Query cache is hydrated from the server's dehydrated state. Subsequent navigations use client-side caching. The tradeoff: more complex setup than pure Server Components but enables optimistic updates, background refetching, and all TanStack Query features.

Next.js streaming with Suspense: wrapping slow data-fetching components in Suspense enables progressive page rendering. Fast content renders first; slow content streams in later. But Suspense requires the component to actually 'suspend' — using useEffect for data fetching doesn't suspend. UNSAID: Suspense works with: async Server Components (they suspend until the async function resolves), `use(promise)` hook in Client Components (React 19), and TanStack Query's `useSuspenseQuery`. Regular `useEffect + useState` patterns do NOT suspend — they render immediately with undefined state, then update. For proper streaming, switch from useEffect fetching to async Server Components or useSuspenseQuery. The loading.tsx file creates an automatic Suspense boundary for the entire route segment.

Next.js with Clerk for authentication: Clerk provides prebuilt UI components (SignIn, SignUp, UserButton) and hooks (useAuth, useUser). Middleware setup: `authMiddleware()` from @clerk/nextjs wraps the app and protects routes. `auth()` in Server Components retrieves session. UNSAID: Clerk handles ALL auth complexity: email/password, magic links, OAuth (30+ providers), SMS OTP, MFA, session management, and org/team management. The tradeoff: Clerk is a paid service (~$25/mo for small projects, scalable). The alternatives: NextAuth (free, self-managed), Supabase Auth (free tier), Auth0 (expensive at scale). For teams without dedicated auth engineers, Clerk's ROI is positive — building equivalent features takes 2-4 weeks of engineering time.

Next.js production deployment checklist: (1) Environment variables set in hosting platform (Vercel, Railway, etc.), (2) `NEXTAUTH_SECRET` (or `AUTH_SECRET`) set for auth session encryption, (3) Database connection string pointing to production DB (not dev), (4) Image remote patterns configured in next.config.js for external image hosts, (5) CSP headers configured, (6) Rate limiting on API routes, (7) Error monitoring (Sentry) integrated, (8) Analytics (Vercel Analytics, Plausible) added. UNSAID: the most common production launch failure: forgetting to set `NEXTAUTH_SECRET` — causes all auth sessions to fail silently (users can't log in). The second most common: database URL pointing to dev database — all production data goes to the wrong place.

Next.js Script component optimization: `strategy='lazyOnload'` defers script execution until browser idle. `strategy='afterInteractive'` executes after page becomes interactive (after hydration). `strategy='beforeInteractive'` blocks parsing (use only for critical scripts like consent management). `strategy='worker'` runs in a Web Worker (experimental, Partytown integration). UNSAID: the Strategy controls WHEN the script executes relative to React hydration. afterInteractive is correct for analytics scripts — they should execute after users can interact. lazyOnload is for non-critical scripts (chat widgets, social embeds) that shouldn't compete with page interactivity. The `onLoad` and `onReady` callbacks fire after the script loads — use for initializing libraries that depend on script execution.

Next.js App Router: using `useState` or `useEffect` in a Server Component causes a build error: 'You're importing a component that needs useState/useEffect. This only works in a Client Component'. The fix is to add 'use client' to the component. UNSAID: this error is the most common App Router learning curve moment. The mental model: Server Components have no lifecycle, no state, no hooks — they're async functions that return JSX. Anything that needs interactivity or browser APIs needs to be a Client Component. The correct architecture: Server Component fetches data and renders layout; Client Components (with 'use client') handle interactive parts. Move the minimal interactive portion to a separate Client Component file rather than marking the entire page as client.

Next.js error.tsx: Error Boundary for App Router route segments. `'use client'` is required (Error Boundaries are class components internally). Receives `error` and `reset` props. `reset()` retries rendering the route segment. UNSAID: error.tsx automatically wraps the route segment's page.tsx and layout.tsx in an Error Boundary. Errors thrown in Server Components during rendering are caught by the nearest error.tsx above. The `global-error.tsx` in the app/ root handles errors in the root layout. Without error.tsx, unhandled server rendering errors crash the entire page — error.tsx provides graceful degradation. The `digest` property on the error object is a server-side error ID — used to correlate Sentry errors with Next.js server logs without exposing error details to the client.

Next.js caching strategy layers: Request Memoization (per-render deduplication), Data Cache (server-side persistent, configured per fetch), Full Route Cache (static HTML at build time), Router Cache (client-side navigation cache). These four layers stack — a fetch can be memoized AND cached in the Data Cache AND cached as static HTML. UNSAID: the complexity of Next.js 14 caching was widely criticized — too many interacting layers with non-intuitive defaults. Next.js 15 changed defaults: fetch is now `no-store` by default (dynamic by default), requiring opt-in for caching. This reversed the Next.js 14 default (cached by default). The change was made because too many developers accidentally served stale data by forgetting to opt-out of caching.

Next.js redirect() and notFound() throw special errors: `redirect()` throws `NEXT_REDIRECT`, `notFound()` throws `NEXT_NOT_FOUND`. These must NOT be called inside try/catch blocks — the catch block will catch and swallow the error, preventing the redirect/not-found from happening. UNSAID: this is a common Server Action and Server Component bug. Pattern that works: `if (!user) notFound(); return <UserPage user={user} />;`. Pattern that fails: `try { const user = await getUser(); if (!user) notFound(); ... } catch (e) { console.log('error') }` — the catch catches NEXT_NOT_FOUND. The fix: check conditions outside try/catch, or check the error type in catch: `if (e.message === 'NEXT_NOT_FOUND') throw e;`.

Next.js with Vercel Analytics and Speed Insights: `@vercel/analytics` provides real user monitoring (RUM) for Core Web Vitals. Add `<Analytics />` to root layout. Vercel Speed Insights tracks performance with real user data. UNSAID: Vercel Analytics is free on all Vercel plans. It respects user privacy (no cookies, GDPR-compliant by default). Speed Insights samples performance metrics from real users, showing p50/p75/p95 LCP, FID, and CLS. The data is more accurate than Lighthouse (which uses simulated throttling) because it uses actual user devices and network conditions. For non-Vercel deployments, Vercel Analytics is not available — use alternatives: Plausible (privacy-first), PostHog (open-source, self-hostable), or Google Analytics 4.

Next.js App Router React DevTools shows Server Components in the component tree but they appear without state or hooks. Attempting to inspect Server Component props in DevTools may not show all server-side data (some is omitted for security). UNSAID: Server Component debugging is primarily server-side. console.log in Server Components outputs to the terminal/server logs, not the browser console. Next.js 14+ forwards server console output to the browser console in development — a significant DX improvement. In production, server logs must be accessed via the hosting platform's log aggregator (Vercel Functions logs, AWS CloudWatch). The stack trace on server errors includes the original TypeScript source when source maps are generated — ensure source maps are enabled in next.config.js.

Next.js with Drizzle ORM: type-safe SQL queries with TypeScript inference. `db.select().from(users).where(eq(users.id, id))` produces typed results. Schema defined in TypeScript, migrations generated with `drizzle-kit generate`. UNSAID: Drizzle ORM is the lightweight alternative to Prisma — no Prisma Client generation, no Prisma binary, direct SQL semantics. Drizzle's API is closer to SQL than Prisma's (which has a custom query language). Performance: Drizzle produces cleaner SQL with fewer unnecessary JOINs than Prisma in some cases. The choice between Drizzle and Prisma: Prisma has better documentation and ecosystem; Drizzle has better performance for complex queries and smaller bundle size. With Next.js serverless, Drizzle's lack of a binary is an advantage (Prisma's binary adds ~10MB to function bundles).

Next.js Vercel deployment: the `.next` directory is the build output — never commit it to git. The `public` directory is served statically. Files in public/ are accessible at the root URL path: `public/image.png` → `/image.png`. UNSAID: the common production performance issue: large files accidentally placed in public/ (unoptimized images, unminified JS) are served without optimization. Next.js Image component optimization only applies to images imported via next/image, not directly accessed from public/. For user-uploaded assets: use S3/R2/Cloudflare Images with CDN distribution, not Next.js public/ directory. The public/ directory is appropriate for: favicon.ico, robots.txt, sitemap.xml, and static assets that don't need processing.

Next.js internationalization (i18n) with next-intl: middleware detects locale from URL path, cookies, or accept-language header. `routing.ts` defines supported locales and default locale. `[locale]/layout.tsx` wraps the localized content. Server Components use `getTranslations()` (async); Client Components use `useTranslations()`. UNSAID: next-intl's routing approach (locale in URL path: /en/about, /fr/about) is SEO-optimal — each locale has its own URL, crawled independently. The middleware uses unstable_setRequestLocale (server-side locale setting without await) for static page generation. Without unstable_setRequestLocale, all localized pages render dynamically (SSR instead of SSG). JSON translation files support nested keys, pluralization (ICU format with @formatjs/icu-messageformat-parser), and variable interpolation.

Next.js experimental React Compiler support: enable in next.config.js with `experimental: { reactCompiler: true }`. React Compiler automatically memoizes components, eliminating manual React.memo/useMemo/useCallback. UNSAID: React Compiler in Next.js is the path of least resistance for adopting it — Next.js handles the compiler plugin configuration. Components that violate React's rules of hooks or render purity are automatically skipped by the compiler (not broken, just not optimized). The compiler's benefit in Next.js: reduces client-side JavaScript by eliminating redundant re-renders. The improvement is most noticeable in complex interactive dashboards with many stateful components. Monitor bundle size before and after enabling to verify no regressions.

Next.js App Router project structure best practices: `app/` for routes, `components/` for shared UI, `lib/` for utilities, `hooks/` for custom hooks, `stores/` for Zustand/Jotai stores, `server/` for server-only utilities (db, auth), `types/` for TypeScript types. UNSAID: the `server/` directory (or `lib/server/`) with `import 'server-only'` at the top prevents importing server-only modules in Client Components. `import 'server-only'` throws a build error if the module is imported in a Client Component — enforcing the security boundary. The `components/ui/` subdirectory convention (shadcn/ui default) separates design system primitives from feature-specific components in `components/features/`.

Next.js 15 React 19 compatibility: Next.js 15 ships with React 19 RC. Third-party libraries that haven't been updated for React 19 may fail due to: (1) Deprecated APIs removal (string refs, legacy context), (2) Changed behavior of strict mode effects, (3) New peer dependency requirements. UNSAID: check library compatibility with React 19 before upgrading to Next.js 15. The Next.js upgrade guide lists known incompatible libraries. React 19 removes UNSAFE_ lifecycle methods entirely (they were deprecated in React 16 with warnings). Libraries using these methods will throw at runtime. Running `npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest` applies automated migration transforms.

Next.js with Payload CMS: self-hosted headless CMS that runs inside Next.js. The Payload admin UI and API are mounted at `/admin` and `/api` within the same Next.js application. Payload manages content types (posts, users, media) with an auto-generated admin UI. UNSAID: Payload CMS colocation with Next.js eliminates the decoupled headless CMS architecture — no separate CMS server, no CMS API calls from Next.js. The content database (MongoDB or PostgreSQL) is shared. This is the 'full-stack CMS' approach: content, admin, and frontend in one deployed application. The tradeoff: tighter coupling between CMS and frontend, harder to swap one without the other. For teams comfortable with this coupling, the operational simplicity (one deployment, one database) is significant.

Next.js with Sentry error monitoring: `@sentry/nextjs` instruments both server and client. `withSentryConfig` wraps next.config.js to enable source map upload, server component tracing, and Sentry SDK initialization. `sentry.server.config.ts` and `sentry.client.config.ts` for separate configurations. UNSAID: the Sentry Next.js SDK instruments: Server Components rendering errors, API route errors, client-side React errors (via Error Boundary), and performance tracing for all routes. The `tunnel` option in Sentry config routes events through your own Next.js API route instead of directly to Sentry — bypasses ad blockers that block sentry.io. Source maps uploaded to Sentry (via `sentry.hideSourceMaps: true` to keep them off CDN) enable readable stack traces in the Sentry dashboard.

Next.js Pages Router getServerSideProps vs App Router: getServerSideProps runs on every request, blocks the entire page render until data is fetched. App Router Server Components are more granular — only the components that fetch data await, others render in parallel. UNSAID: this is the fundamental performance improvement of App Router over Pages Router for data-heavy pages. A Pages Router page with 3 data sources (user, posts, notifications) must fetch all three sequentially or handle parallel fetching manually with Promise.all. App Router Server Components: each fetches independently in parallel, and Suspense boundaries allow partial streaming. The waterfall of sequential fetches is architecturally eliminated by the component-based model.

Next.js OpenGraph image generation: `app/opengraph-image.tsx` exports a default function that returns `ImageResponse` from @vercel/og. The function can be async and fetch data. Generated at build time for static routes, on-demand for dynamic routes. UNSAID: @vercel/og uses Satori (HTML/CSS to SVG) + resvg (SVG to PNG). Supports most Tailwind CSS classes and inline styles. Limitations: no CSS Grid, no @font-face loaded via CSS (use the `fonts` option in ImageResponse instead), and viewport is fixed (1200x630 for Open Graph). Dynamic OG images enable: personalized share cards with user names, data-driven charts as images, and blog post previews with cover images — all generated server-side without Canvas libraries.

Next.js router.push vs Link component: `router.push()` programmatic navigation vs `<Link>` declarative navigation. Link prefetches linked pages when they appear in the viewport — router.push does not prefetch. For SEO: Link renders as an `<a>` tag, crawlable by bots. UNSAID: using `router.push()` everywhere instead of `<Link>` causes: no prefetching (slower navigation), no right-click 'Open in new tab', no bot crawlability, and breaks browser history expectations. Use `<Link>` for all navigational links (menu items, buttons that navigate). Use `router.push()` only for programmatic navigation after events (form submit → redirect, error → redirect to login). The `replace` prop on Link (and `router.replace()`) replaces the current history entry instead of adding — appropriate for redirect-after-form-submit patterns.

Next.js with AI integration: `ai` package (Vercel AI SDK) provides `streamText` (server) and `useChat` / `useCompletion` (client hooks) for building AI-powered features. Route handler: `const result = await streamText({ model, messages }); return result.toDataStreamResponse()`. UNSAID: Vercel AI SDK handles streaming protocol, tool calls, and structured output. The `toDataStreamResponse()` returns a streaming Response compatible with the client-side `useChat` hook — they share a protocol. Multi-model support: OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, Google Gemini, and open-source models via Ollama. The `generateObject` function generates JSON matching a Zod schema using structured output (GPT-4/Claude's function calling). Rate limiting AI routes: use Upstash Redis rate limiting in middleware or route handlers.

Next.js form handling with React 19 actions: HTML forms with `action={serverAction}` submit directly to server actions without client JavaScript. `useFormStatus()` inside the form provides `pending` state for disabling the submit button. `useActionState()` provides the action result and pending state. UNSAID: this is the progressive enhancement pattern — the form works without JavaScript (submits as a regular HTML form to the server action) and is enhanced with JavaScript (loading states, optimistic updates). For accessibility, forms must work without JavaScript by default. The 'no JavaScript' form submission requires the server action to handle redirects (using redirect()) and validation errors (returning structured error data from the action). React 19 + Next.js 15 make this pattern first-class.

Next.js `dynamic('...', { ssr: false })` disables server-side rendering for a component — useful for components that use browser APIs (localStorage, window, DOM). But it adds JavaScript bundle overhead and causes a loading flash on initial render. UNSAID: `ssr: false` is the correct fix for hydration errors from browser-only components, but it's a tradeoff. The component doesn't render during SSR, so the page HTML is missing that content — affecting SEO for SEO-critical content and causing a content layout shift (CLS) for below-the-fold content. For components that access browser APIs, check if the API can be accessed after mount (in useEffect) instead of during render. If so, keep SSR enabled and guard the API access with `typeof window !== 'undefined'`.

Next.js with Cloudflare: OpenNext.js adapter enables Next.js on Cloudflare Pages and Workers. The adapter converts Next.js build output to Cloudflare Workers-compatible format. Edge runtime pages run at 300+ Cloudflare edge locations. UNSAID: OpenNext is a community project (now sponsored by SST) that makes Next.js work on non-Vercel infrastructure. Cloudflare's official Next.js support uses OpenNext internally. The tradeoff vs Vercel: Cloudflare is significantly cheaper for high traffic (Workers pricing is request-based with generous free tier), but some Next.js features (Image optimization, ISR) require workarounds. R2 (Cloudflare's S3-compatible storage) is the natural companion for user uploads in Cloudflare-deployed Next.js apps.

Next.js App Router TypeScript: page components MUST export a default function, not a named export. `export default function Page({ params }) { ... }` — `export function Page()` is NOT recognized as a route page by the router. UNSAID: this is a subtle Next.js constraint that causes silent failures — a named export doesn't throw an error, it just shows a 404 or the default not-found page because Next.js can't find the page component. The same applies to layout.tsx, loading.tsx, and error.tsx — all must use default exports. The TypeScript type for page props is `{ params: { [key: string]: string }; searchParams: { [key: string]: string } }` — both params and searchParams are plain string objects (not URLSearchParams).

Next.js CI/CD pipeline: (1) `tsc --noEmit` type check, (2) `next lint` (ESLint with Next.js rules), (3) `vitest run` unit tests, (4) `next build` production build (catches SSG/SSR errors), (5) Playwright E2E tests against the production build. UNSAID: `next build` does more than compile — it runs generateStaticParams for SSG routes, executes all async server component fetches for static pages, and catches runtime errors that TypeScript wouldn't catch. CI failures at the `next build` step often reveal missing environment variables, database connectivity issues, or incorrect data transformations. Running `next build` in CI with the same env vars as production is the highest-value CI check.

Next.js with Sanity CMS: Sanity Studio runs separately or can be embedded in Next.js. `@sanity/client` fetches content via GROQ queries. Live Preview with `@sanity/next-sanity` enables draft content preview in Next.js. UNSAID: Sanity's Live Preview requires Next.js Draft Mode — a special cookie that switches Next.js pages from cached (public) to uncached (preview) mode. The route handler `/api/draft/enable` sets the Draft Mode cookie via `draftMode().enable()`. Pages check `draftMode().isEnabled` to decide whether to fetch published or draft content. This is the standard headless CMS preview architecture: published content served statically, draft content served dynamically with auth. Sanity's 'Live Preview' (real-time updates as editors type) uses a streaming connection.

Next.js Turbopack (development bundler): Turbopack replaces webpack in Next.js 15 development mode by default. Claims 10-700x faster HMR than webpack. Enabled with `next dev --turbopack` in Next.js 14, default in Next.js 15. UNSAID: Turbopack does NOT replace webpack for production builds yet (as of 2024) — production builds still use webpack. The performance difference is most noticeable in large projects where webpack's module resolution becomes slow. Turbopack is written in Rust (same as esbuild/swc) — the speed comes from native compilation and more efficient module caching. Some webpack plugins and configurations are not yet supported by Turbopack — check compatibility before enabling. Vite-based projects don't need Turbopack (Vite is already fast for SPA development).

Next.js useSearchParams() must be wrapped in Suspense on the client: it opts out of static rendering. In Next.js 14+, components using `useSearchParams()` without a Suspense boundary cause the entire route to render dynamically. UNSAID: Next.js 14's build warning: 'useSearchParams() should be wrapped in a suspense boundary at page or a component level. Read more: ...' — this is easy to miss. The fix: wrap the component using useSearchParams in `<Suspense fallback={null}>`. This makes only that component dynamic (streaming) while the rest of the page remains static. The same applies to `usePathname()` in some configurations. The pattern: isolate URL-reading hooks in small components wrapped with Suspense to maintain static rendering for the majority of the page.

Next.js sitemap generation: `app/sitemap.ts` exports a default function returning an array of `{ url, lastModified, changeFrequency, priority }` objects. For dynamic routes, fetch all URLs from the database and include them. UNSAID: Next.js generates `sitemap.xml` at build time from the exported array. For large sitemaps (50,000+ URLs), use multiple sitemap files: `app/sitemap/[id]/route.ts` generates paginated sitemaps referenced by a sitemap index. The `changeFrequency` and `priority` fields influence crawl scheduling — set `changeFrequency: 'daily'` for frequently-updated content, `'monthly'` for stable pages. `lastModified` helps search engines prioritize recrawling recently-changed content. Nuxt has built-in sitemap module; Next.js requires manual implementation or `next-sitemap` package.

Next.js App Router React Context: Context providers must be Client Components ('use client'). A common pattern is wrapping the app in multiple Context providers, all needing 'use client'. This makes the entire subtree Client Components if the providers are in layout.tsx. UNSAID: the fix is the 'provider wrapper' pattern: create a ClientProviders.tsx file with 'use client' that contains all Context providers, then import it in the Server Component layout.tsx. The layout.tsx remains a Server Component (can fetch data) while ClientProviders.tsx handles the client-side context tree. This is the standard pattern for: Redux Provider, TanStack Query QueryClientProvider, Auth providers, and Theme providers in App Router.

Next.js with Upstash for rate limiting and caching: `@upstash/ratelimit` + Redis for rate limiting API routes and server actions. `@upstash/redis` for server-side caching. Upstash provides serverless Redis — pay-per-request, no persistent connections. UNSAID: traditional Redis clients (ioredis) use TCP connections that don't work in serverless/edge environments (Vercel Functions, Cloudflare Workers). Upstash Redis uses HTTP — compatible with any runtime. The rate limiting pattern: `ratelimit.limit(identifier)` returns `{ success, limit, remaining, reset }` — respond with 429 if `!success`. For Next.js middleware rate limiting: use the Edge-compatible Upstash client (not ioredis). The cost: Upstash's HTTP overhead per request is ~5-10ms vs TCP Redis's <1ms for local Redis.

Next.js debugging production issues: (1) Check Vercel function logs for server errors, (2) Use Sentry for structured error reporting with source maps, (3) Enable `experimental.instrumentationHook` for OpenTelemetry tracing, (4) Check Vercel's build logs for SSG/ISR failures, (5) Test with `next build && next start` locally before deploying. UNSAID: production-only bugs often stem from: (1) Environment variable differences between local and production, (2) Node.js version differences (specify in package.json engines), (3) Edge vs Node.js runtime API differences, (4) Database connection limits being hit in production traffic. The `next start` command runs the production build locally — essential for catching production-mode-specific issues before deployment.

Next.js with Neon Postgres: serverless PostgreSQL with auto-suspend (scales to zero, resumes in milliseconds). The `@neondatabase/serverless` driver uses WebSockets instead of TCP — compatible with Edge runtime. Drizzle ORM or Prisma work with Neon. UNSAID: Neon's branching feature enables database branching for PRs — each pull request gets its own database branch (like git branches for data). This eliminates the need for separate staging databases. Neon's serverless auto-suspend is ideal for development/staging where uptime 24/7 is unnecessary — cost reduction of 70-90% vs always-on Postgres. The cold start on suspend (5-10 seconds) is acceptable for development but not production — disable auto-suspend for production or use a dedicated instance.

Next.js App Router rehydration of URL search params: `searchParams` in Server Component `page.tsx` props is a plain object at build time in static pages, but a dynamic value per-request in SSR pages. Accessing `searchParams` opts the page into dynamic rendering. UNSAID: this is a common accidental dynamic rendering trigger. A static page that filters by URL query param (`?category=shoes`) becomes fully dynamic because searchParams changes per-request. The fix for maintaining static rendering with query-param-driven filtering: handle filtering client-side using the URL params (read with useSearchParams on the client) without accessing searchParams in the Server Component. The server renders the full unfiltered dataset statically; the client filters/displays based on URL params.

Next.js A/B testing with middleware: Middleware reads a cookie to determine the variant, assigns a random variant for new visitors, sets the cookie, rewrites the request to the variant page. All without JavaScript on the client. UNSAID: middleware-based A/B testing is the most performant approach — variants are served from distinct static pages (no client-side conditional rendering), and the assignment happens at the CDN edge before content delivery. The cookie ensures consistent variant assignment across the session. Vercel's Edge Config provides millisecond reads of A/B test configuration (no database round-trip). Statistical significance monitoring requires an analytics service (PostHog, Amplitude) tracking variant assignments and conversions.

Next.js App Router and client-side navigation: `router.refresh()` re-runs Server Components for the current route, fetching fresh data from the server without full page reload. `router.push()` navigates without refresh. Calling router.refresh() after a mutation ensures Server Component data is up-to-date. UNSAID: the difference between router.refresh() and revalidatePath(): router.refresh() refreshes for the current user's browser (client-side); revalidatePath() clears the server-side cache for all users (server-side). Using router.refresh() instead of revalidatePath() for multi-user apps shows the current user fresh data but other users still see stale cached data. For multi-user data consistency, use revalidatePath()/revalidateTag() in server actions.

Next.js's decision to default to server-rendering with App Router (vs client-rendering with Pages Router) reflects the industry realization that SPAs often over-delivered on interactivity at the cost of SEO, performance, and complexity. The pendulum swung: MPA (server-rendered) → SPA (client-rendered) → islands architecture → streaming SSR. Next.js App Router is the current equilibrium: server by default, client where needed, streaming for latency. UNSAID: this architectural choice means Next.js 14 apps more closely resemble PHP/Rails applications in data flow (request → server process → HTML response) than traditional React SPAs (empty HTML → client fetches → client renders). The mental model shift is significant — developers need to think 'where does this code run' for every component.

Next.js with Posthog for product analytics: client-side event tracking with PostHog React provider. `PostHogProvider` wraps the app. `usePostHog()` hook for imperative event tracking. Feature flags via `useFeatureFlagEnabled()`. UNSAID: PostHog is the open-source alternative to Amplitude, Mixpanel, and LaunchDarkly combined — analytics, feature flags, session recordings, A/B tests, and funnels in one tool. Self-hosted option (PostHog Cloud or Docker) provides full data ownership. For GDPR compliance: enable data anonymization and cookie-less tracking. Session recordings (screen recording of user sessions) are PostHog's most powerful debugging feature — seeing exactly what a user did before a bug report. Next.js PostHog integration: server-side event tracking via PostHog's Node.js library in API routes and server actions.

Next.js Image optimization for external images: `next.config.js` must list all external domains in `images.remotePatterns`. Missing configuration causes: 'Error: Invalid src prop, hostname is not configured under images in your next.config.js'. UNSAID: this security check prevents your Next.js image optimization endpoint from being used as a proxy for arbitrary URLs (abuse vector). The `remotePatterns` config with `protocol`, `hostname`, `port`, and `pathname` glob patterns gives precise control. For user-generated content with multiple CDN domains, use the `remotePatterns: [{ hostname: '**.cloudfront.net' }]` wildcard pattern. When using an image CDN (Cloudinary, Imgix), configure next.config.js `images.loader` and `images.loaderFile` to use the CDN's URL generation instead of Next.js's built-in optimization.

Next.js with Resend for transactional email: `resend.emails.send({ from, to, subject, react: <EmailTemplate /> })`. React Email provides React components for email templates with Tailwind CSS support. UNSAID: sending email from Server Actions or API routes requires: (1) Resend API key in server-side environment variable (non-NEXT_PUBLIC_), (2) Domain verification in Resend dashboard (can send from your domain), (3) Rate limiting to prevent email abuse. React Email's components (Html, Head, Body, Text, Button, Image, Hr) produce cross-client-compatible email HTML — the hardest part of email development. Resend's webhook integration (`/api/webhooks/resend`) receives delivery status (delivered, bounced, spam reported) for monitoring email deliverability.

Next.js Server Action security: Server Actions are automatically secured with an CSRF token (SameSite cookie + origin check), but they are PUBLIC HTTP endpoints. Any data processing must be validated server-side. Don't assume client-side validation protects server actions. UNSAID: the CSRF protection prevents cross-site form submissions, but it doesn't prevent: (1) Malicious users calling the action with crafted payloads, (2) Horizontal privilege escalation (user A calling action that should only affect user B), (3) Missing authentication checks (anyone calling the action without being logged in). Every Server Action must: validate input with Zod, check authentication (verify session), and check authorization (verify the user has permission for this specific resource). The pattern: `const session = await auth(); if (!session) throw new Error('Unauthorized');`.

Next.js App Router starter template: `npx create-next-app@latest --typescript --tailwind --eslint --app --src-dir --import-alias @/*`. This scaffolds: App Router, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, ESLint, src/ directory, and path aliases. Then add: shadcn/ui (`npx shadcn@latest init`), Auth.js, Prisma/Drizzle, Vitest, Playwright. UNSAID: the `--src-dir` flag places all source code in `src/` instead of the root — separates source from config files. `--import-alias @/*` configures both tsconfig.json and the bundler for `@/components/ui` style imports. The shadcn/ui init command reads the Next.js config to determine the correct path alias and component installation location. Starting from create-next-app instead of manual setup avoids 15+ manual configuration steps.

Next.js build optimization: `analyze: true` in next.config.js runs @next/bundle-analyzer to visualize the bundle. Large bundles are caused by: importing entire libraries (lodash instead of lodash/pick), missing code splitting, and including server-only code in client bundles. UNSAID: the bundle analyzer shows TWO views: server bundle (page.tsx server component tree) and client bundle (client components + js). The client bundle is what users download — focus optimization here. Common offenders: date-fns imported entirely (~60KB) vs specific functions (~5KB), moment.js (~300KB), and large icon libraries (importing all icons instead of specific ones). The Next.js-specific bundle issue: accidentally including Prisma client or other Node.js-only modules in the client bundle — these cause build errors or runtime failures in the browser.

Next.js with Stripe webhooks: route handler at `/api/webhooks/stripe` verifies webhook signature (`stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(body, signature, secret)`), processes events, and returns 200. Must use `export const runtime = 'nodejs'` (webhook verification requires raw body, not JSON-parsed). UNSAID: the critical implementation detail: Next.js route handlers parse the request body by default. Stripe webhook verification needs the RAW body (Buffer), not parsed JSON. Access raw body with `await request.arrayBuffer()` then `Buffer.from(arrayBuffer)`. Missing this causes webhook signature verification failures. The webhook endpoint must be added to Stripe's dashboard settings AND verified in the Stripe CLI with `stripe listen --forward-to localhost:3000/api/webhooks/stripe` for local testing.

Next.js Instrumentation: `instrumentation.ts` in the root provides a server startup hook. `register()` function runs once when the Next.js server starts. Use for: OpenTelemetry SDK initialization, Sentry SDK initialization, database connection pooling setup. UNSAID: instrumentation.ts is the correct place for SDK initialization that should happen once per server startup — not in every route or API call. The `register()` function runs in both Node.js and Edge runtimes — check the runtime with `process.env.NEXT_RUNTIME` ('nodejs' or 'edge') to apply runtime-specific setup. Next.js 15 stabilized instrumentation.ts (was experimental in 14). This is important for OpenTelemetry tracing setup that must happen before any requests are processed.

Next.js Pages Router vs App Router: getStaticProps, getServerSideProps, and getInitialProps do NOT exist in App Router. Migrating from Pages Router: replace `getServerSideProps` with async Server Component, replace `getStaticProps` with static Server Component + `generateStaticParams`, replace `getStaticPaths` with `generateStaticParams`. UNSAID: the mental model shift is significant. Pages Router passes data via props from data-fetching functions to the page component (separate concerns). App Router data fetching is colocated in the component that needs it (unified concern). Many teams migrate incrementally — pages/ and app/ can coexist during migration. The App Router has no equivalent for `getInitialProps` (which ran on both server and client) — use Server Components for server-fetched data and client-side hooks for client-only data.

Next.js with Supabase: `@supabase/ssr` package provides server-side Supabase client that reads cookies correctly in Server Components, Server Actions, and Route Handlers. `createClient()` from @supabase/ssr creates a server-side client. UNSAID: the older `@supabase/auth-helpers-nextjs` package is deprecated — migrate to `@supabase/ssr`. The critical distinction: browser-side Supabase client uses localStorage for session; server-side Supabase client reads cookies (forwarded by `@supabase/ssr`). Middleware must refresh the session cookie: `await supabase.auth.getSession()` in middleware updates the session if expired. Without middleware session refresh, users get logged out after the JWT expires even if they're actively using the app.

Next.js TypeScript and module resolution: `@` path alias works in .ts/.tsx files with tsconfig paths. But in non-TypeScript contexts (MDX, CSS Modules importing from JS), the alias may not resolve. UNSAID: the `@` alias requires both tsconfig.json `paths` AND the bundler's alias configuration to be synchronized. With Next.js, the tsconfig paths are automatically read by the Next.js webpack/turbopack configuration. The failure mode: a new team member adds a custom path alias to tsconfig without updating next.config.js — TypeScript shows no errors but the build fails. The safe pattern: only use the `@/` alias defined by create-next-app, which is guaranteed to work with Next.js's bundler.

Next.js with Trigger.dev for background jobs: long-running tasks (AI processing, report generation, email batch sends) that can't fit in serverless function timeout (10 seconds on Vercel hobby, 60 seconds on Pro). `trigger.defineJob()` creates a job, `client.sendEvent()` triggers it. UNSAID: Vercel function timeout is the most common reason teams need a background job service. The alternatives: Inngest (event-driven functions), Trigger.dev (background jobs with retries and observability), QStash (serverless queue). All three work with Next.js serverless. The pattern: Next.js server action validates input, enqueues the job, returns immediately with a job ID; the background service processes the job and updates the database; the client polls for completion or uses WebSocket for real-time updates.

Next.js's positioning in the React ecosystem: it's the official React framework recommended by the React team. Not a community project — Vercel employs significant portions of the React core team. Architectural decisions in Next.js (App Router, Server Components, Server Actions) are co-designed with the React team and represent the future direction of React itself. UNSAID: the React team's recommendation of Next.js over plain React reflects the realization that raw React requires too much configuration (routing, data fetching, SSR) for most production applications. The React docs now guide new learners toward frameworks (Next.js, Remix) rather than raw React + Vite. This creates a feedback loop: React innovations (Server Components) are designed with Next.js in mind, making Next.js the best environment to use them.

Next.js middleware and i18n: locale detection in middleware reads `Accept-Language` header and `NEXT_LOCALE` cookie. The rewrite from `/` to `/en` must be done carefully to avoid redirect loops. Next.js 13+ has built-in i18n routing support in next.config.js (`i18n: { locales, defaultLocale }`) for Pages Router. App Router does NOT have built-in i18n routing — use next-intl's middleware instead. UNSAID: the removal of built-in i18n from App Router was controversial. The Pages Router's i18n routing was simple to configure; App Router requires a third-party solution (next-intl or manual middleware). The next-intl middleware approach is more flexible but requires understanding middleware rewriting, locale detection order, and default locale handling.

Next.js with tRPC: tRPC router defined in server/ directory with procedures. HTTP adapter mounts router at `/api/trpc`. Client configured with `createTRPCReact()`. In App Router, tRPC Server-Side Helpers call procedures without HTTP — useful in Server Components. UNSAID: tRPC with App Router has two patterns: (1) Client Components use the tRPC React Query client (network calls with caching), (2) Server Components use tRPC's server caller (direct function calls, no HTTP). Combining both: Server Component prefetches data using the server caller, Client Component's React Query hydrates from the prefetched data. This gives SSR for initial load and client-side caching for subsequent interactions. The type safety flows from tRPC router types to both server and client without code generation.

Next.js 13 App Router early adoption mistakes (historical): the early App Router had significant bugs (router cache not clearing, incorrect static/dynamic detection, revalidation not working). Many teams adopted too early and encountered framework-level bugs. UNSAID: the lesson: next@14 (stable App Router) is the recommended adoption point. The App Router's stability story: Next.js 13 (experimental), Next.js 13.4 (stable but with known issues), Next.js 14 (production-ready). Teams that adopted Next.js 13 App Router in production in early 2023 spent significant time working around framework bugs rather than shipping features. The current state (2024): App Router is production-ready, but always check the Next.js GitHub issues before adopting new experimental features.

Next.js performance monitoring checklist: (1) Vercel Analytics for Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, FID/INP), (2) Bundle analyzer for JS size, (3) Lighthouse audit for individual pages, (4) Network tab analysis of waterfall (parallel vs sequential requests), (5) Server-side timing with OpenTelemetry traces. UNSAID: the most impactful Next.js performance improvements in order: (1) Move data fetching to Server Components (eliminates client-server waterfall), (2) Add route-level static generation where possible, (3) Add `priority` to above-the-fold images (LCP improvement), (4) Add font optimization with `next/font` (CLS improvement), (5) Implement code splitting for large pages. Start with Core Web Vitals — they directly impact Google search ranking.

Next.js with Kinde Auth: authentication SaaS with Next.js integration. `getKindeServerSession()` provides session in Server Components. `KindeProvider` wraps the app. Social logins, passwordless, and enterprise SSO out of the box. UNSAID: Kinde is positioned as a Clerk competitor with a more generous free tier. The API surface is similar — session management, user management, organization management. The decision between Kinde, Clerk, and Auth.js: (1) Kinde: generous free tier, simpler pricing at scale, slightly less polished UI components, (2) Clerk: better UI components, more features, more expensive at scale, (3) Auth.js: free, self-managed, requires more implementation work. All three work well with Next.js App Router.

Next.js Edge Runtime limitations in route handlers: Edge Runtime doesn't support: `fs` module, `path` module, `crypto` module (use `globalThis.crypto`), `Buffer` (use `Uint8Array`), and most Node.js built-ins. Edge Runtime supports: fetch, Response, Request, Headers, URL, TextEncoder, TextDecoder, ReadableStream, WebCrypto. UNSAID: accidentally using a Node.js API in an Edge route causes a build error: 'Module X is not supported in Edge Runtime'. The fix: either switch to Node.js runtime (`export const runtime = 'nodejs'`) or use the Web API equivalent. The runtime config is per-route: `export const runtime = 'edge'` or `'nodejs'`. Edge Runtime has the performance advantage of running closer to the user globally; Node.js Runtime has the capability advantage of full Node.js APIs.

Next.js with Prisma Accelerate: connection pooling as a service for serverless Next.js deployments. Prisma Accelerate sits between your serverless functions and PostgreSQL, maintaining a connection pool. `DATABASE_URL` points to Prisma Accelerate's URL; `DIRECT_URL` points directly to Postgres for migrations. UNSAID: serverless functions can't maintain database connections between invocations. Each cold start creates a new connection — PostgreSQL has a default max_connections of 100, which is quickly exhausted by concurrent serverless invocations. Prisma Accelerate (or PgBouncer for self-hosted) pools connections server-side — serverless functions connect to the pooler (fast, reused connections) rather than Postgres directly. Without connection pooling, high-traffic Next.js serverless deployments hit `too many connections` errors.

Next.js with Algolia for search: `algoliasearch` client for search queries, `instantsearch.js` or `react-instantsearch` for UI. Index content during CMS writes or via scheduled sync job. Client-side search calls go directly to Algolia (not through Next.js server) — Algolia SDK is safe to use with a public API key. UNSAID: Algolia's 'Search-Only API Key' is safe to expose client-side — it only allows searching, not indexing. The Admin API Key must be server-only (for indexing operations). The indexing pattern for Next.js: database change → Server Action or webhook → Algolia indexing via admin API. For full-text search without external service: Postgres full-text search (`tsvector` + `to_tsquery`) or Meilisearch (self-hosted, Algolia alternative) are cost-effective alternatives.

Next.js hydration mismatch from browser extensions: browser extensions (LastPass, Grammarly, ad blockers, dark mode extensions) inject DOM elements or modify attributes during page load. This causes React hydration mismatches that appear only for users with those extensions installed. UNSAID: these are not bugs in your code — they're environmental issues. React 18 is more resilient to hydration mismatches than React 17 (it recovers instead of crashing), but still logs warnings. Suppressing hydration warnings globally with `data-suppress-hydration-warning` is an anti-pattern — it hides real bugs. The correct approach: add `suppressHydrationWarning` to the `<body>` and `<html>` elements (where extensions inject content), which only suppresses at the top level. Per-element `suppressHydrationWarning` for intentionally browser-modified elements.

Next.js with Vercel KV (Upstash Redis): key-value store for session storage, rate limiting, feature flags, and caching. `@vercel/kv` SDK compatible with `@upstash/redis` API. Available as a Vercel-managed Upstash Redis instance. UNSAID: Vercel KV is Vercel-managed Upstash — same technology, different billing and management. The advantage: zero configuration in Vercel deployments (auto-connected), disadvantage: vendor lock-in to Vercel. The `@vercel/kv` package works with a `KV_URL` environment variable — easy to switch to self-hosted Upstash by changing the URL. Common use cases: rate limiting API routes, storing user sessions (alternative to JWT cookies), caching expensive database queries, and distributed locks for idempotency.

Next.js Server Component async props: TypeScript `params` prop in page components is a Promise in Next.js 15 (due to PPR requirements). `async function Page({ params }: { params: Promise<{ id: string }> }) { const { id } = await params; ... }`. Accessing `params.id` without await causes a TypeScript error. UNSAID: this breaking change from Next.js 14 to 15 affects all dynamic route pages. The upgrade codemod (`@next/codemod`) adds the await automatically. The reason: making params a Promise enables Partial Prerendering (PPR) — the static shell renders without knowing the params, then the dynamic params are awaited for the dynamic content. Legacy behavior (synchronous params) is available in Next.js 15 via the `futureCompatibility` option but will be removed in a future version.

Next.js App Router's choice to make Server Components the default (all components are server unless 'use client') is the opposite of the Pages Router where pages were SSR only if getServerSideProps was used and otherwise SPA-like. This inversion reflects the lessons from SPA overuse — the default should be the more performant option (server-rendered), with explicit opt-in to client interactivity. UNSAID: this default reversal causes the most confusion for developers migrating from Pages Router.
